subject: Statistics
title: The Literary Digest Disaster of 1936
description: A quick, practical guide to The Literary Digest Disaster of 1936.

hook.text: In 1936, a magazine polled 2.4 million people and predicted the wrong president. A 50,000-person poll got it right.

buildup.text: The Literary Digest mailed surveys to car owners and phone subscribers — wealthy Americans, not average ones.

discovery.text: George Gallup used a smaller but representative sample. Size didn't matter — who you asked did.

twist.text: The Digest had 10 million responses. Gallup had 50,000. The smaller sample crushed the larger one.

climax.text: The Literary Digest folded two years later. Gallup founded modern polling. Sampling beat volume.

punchline.text: A biased million can't beat a representative thousand.

quiz.question: Why did the Literary Digest poll fail despite its massive size?
options: It only sampled wealthy Americans — car and phone owners — not the general population
People lied about who they would vote for
